From: _Miasma: Pollution and Purification in Early Greek Religion_; Robert Parker, 1983 , Oxford University Press: New York; Page 177:

"Incense, typical accompaniment of Olympian sacrifice, is not to be used in the cult of the underworld goddess Hecate."

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Makes perfect sense. Thanks for this!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Why does it make perfect sense?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Because ancient Greek religion drew a strong distinction between the Olympian gods, who were above the earth, and the chthonic gods who were below the earth. You worshiped these two classes of deities in very different ways -- for example, you never offered sacrifices of swine to the Olympians, but that was a standard offering to the chthonic deities.

replies.title: menstrual blood
replies.content:
That is an appropriate answer to give to a man.

A woman can use her own menstrual blood to mark territory and reinforce protective wards--this will have an effect on men and boys, no particular effect on other girls and women, and purely physical effects on animals as long as the blood has a scent.

IMO, a woman using her own menstrual blood or a group of women of menstruating age using a pooled supply collected from two or more of them is not restricted to applications relating to death or sterility. It's their own fresh blood, produced by their own healthy bodies. It's simply blood that is being sloughed off because a fertilized egg hasn't implanted in the womb. Menstruating women can use their own menstrual blood whenever the spell calls for freshly drawn human or animal blood as a supply of life force, and the blood is doubly potent because of its potential to nurture new life.

Blood of any kind can be used as a physical link to the person it came from, in the same way as hair, fingernail clippings, a dug up footprint and the like.

replies.title: Re: menstrual blood
replies.content:
JMG. email is a medium that doesn't convey tone of voice well, so I may be reading something into your reply that you didn't intend, but I hear resentment or a rebuke in your reply to me. If so, I don't think it's warranted.

In the first place, what I wrote was not a "smackdown". A smackdown would be my telling you to shut up because menstrual blood is Wimmen's Magic and you have no business opining about it. You will notice that I did not do that. On the contrary, my opening sentence stated that I thought your answer was appropriate to give to the person who asked the question, and my closing sentence expressed agreement that the blood will be personally linked to the person it came from.

In between, I expressed a tempered disagreement with you. I believe I am entitled to do that. You and I have been practicing natural magic for roughly the same number of years. My comments are based in personal experience, which I think should count for something. I have used menstrual blood ritually and magically in the ways I mentioned, alone and in a tight-knit women's coven that flourished in the mid 1970s.

For men, menstrual blood is an exotic ingredient. For most women at a particular stage of our lives, it is a very familiar one which our own bodies produce and shed the way a tree grows and sheds bark. Living with its varied expressions in embodiment, we do not experience female energy in the same way as men do. It takes a tremendous amount of effort and time for most human beings to experience the Other as fully as we do the self.

Moreover, living as we do in a patriarchal society and culture, an important part of the spiritual and magical development of a woman who seeks to be a mage or a witch is to get properly acquainted with the parts of her self that the culture ignores, denies, restricts, undervalues or fears. Magical lore coming from cultural sources that women have not fully participated in creating and transmitting are incomplete. If we women are to live balanced lives and fully access our own talents and perceptions, we cannot rely entirely on the traditions available. We have to do our own work.

The Cabalistic categories that you cite, Marah, Ama and Aimah, are something more than arbitrary intellectual constructs, but they are limited by having arisen in a tradition developed, transmitted and practiced almost entirely by men. Their focus is on what men seek from women and what they fear from us. Furthermore, to state the obvious, the Kabbalah was created by observant Jews. Judaism has very strict blood taboos in general and firm rules requiring the separation of menstruating women from men. Kabbalistic magic is old and powerful, but its practitioners would have regarded any use of menstrual blood as polluting and abhorrent. So they are not the people to go to for advice on this question. Since you pressed me, that's my smackdown.

replies.title: Re: menstrual blood
replies.content: You're right that email is a poor medium for communicating feelings; your original comment came across to me as a patronizing putdown, and I gather that wasn't your intention; fair enough. I should mention, though, that menstrual blood is hardly an exotic ingredient for me, as I've been married for nearly 35 years to a woman who, before menopause finally solved the problem, had fairly bad dysmenorrhea (it ran in her family). There were times when she had flooding problems and was too exhausted to clean herself thoroughly, so I took care of it. (It's just another bodily fluid.) While the version of Cabala we have today got to the Western esoteric tradition by way of Judaism, by the way, it originated in Greek Gnostic circles -- that's what Gershom Scholem showed in his book The Origins of the Kabbalah, in case you're curious -- and since the Renaissance there has been a fully independent Hermetic Cabala which is hardly "developed, transmitted and practiced almost entirely by men" -- I trust you realize that, to name just a few, Moina Mathers, Florence Farr, Dion Fortune, and Dolores Ashcroft-Nowicki all had periods!

Scholem's well worth study in that context. Another I would recommend is John Dillon's The Middle Platonists -- a great deal of Jewish Kabbalah is straight-up Platonism, and such standard Cabalistic concepts as the Primal Worlds occur in Platonist writers such as Plutarch many centuries before they surface in Jewish sources.

"All this puts me in a situation that is frankly rather odd. After all, I’m an occultist—you know, a student and practitioner of traditions of rejected knowledge that claim that under certain circumstances, the mind can do things with its surroundings that the rationalist materialism of our society insists can’t happen. What's occurred to me is that this actually has a very simple cause: one of those things which the human mind is capable of, but our society insists can't happen, is delusion. Our society likes to preen itself on rationality, and so instead of admitting we have flaws and are prone to making incredibly bad decisions and leaps of "logic" that make no sense whatsoever, our culture is happy to go with whatever justifies its bad decisions. I'm convinced confirmation bias, and the other members of the set of known flaws to human thinking play a role here.

Does this logic make sense?

Also, does it perhaps explain why so many people are obsessed with proving occultists are fools/prone to bad logic, since there is a massive flaw hardwired into any attempt at our society's attempt at rationality: our own methods of knowing about existence have shown we are flawed at thinking?

After reading all the other comments to date, it seems that one of my two recurring childhood dreams may belong here, too. At the start of the dream I was crawling up a very long, dimly lit staircase, nearing the top. (I was too young to walk yet.) When I got to the top, a dark hall stretched away indefinitely to the left and the right, like the crossbar on a capital letter T. Whether I turned right or left, the next stage of the dream was much the same, for either way, very large human heads were cemented into the walls on each side of an endless hallway, facing into the hall. The heads were very hungry indeed, and they had long prehensile tongues with which they tried as hard as they could to seize me and devour me. Keeping to the exact center of the hall seemed to be my best chance of not getting eaten. But that was very hard to do, since it was also hard to crawl along a straight line--and also the tongues of the heads weren't all the same length. At some point as I made my way down the dark hall, I would wake up in sheer terror. I was never precisely sure whether or not I had just been caught by one of the heads and was about to be eaten.

Curiously enough, my other recurring childhood dream (at roughly the same age) involved being eaten, too. In it, again I was a crawling child. Eventually I got trapped inside a pig's carcass and sent alive through a meat grinding machine in a slaughter-house, to be made into food for unwitting purchasers. That's clearly not the same class of dream as the first, apart from the sheer level of terror that each of them produced in me every time it recurred. The meat-grinder dream, if it is more than just a nightmare, might actually be a memory of how a previous life of mine had ended; I'm not sure, one way or the other. The heads-in-the-walls dream, however, still feels like it comes from some other source not of this material world.

My dreams tend to be fleeting, and I tend not to remember them very long after waking up. These two, however, are still seared into my memory, both visually and emotionally, almost 75 years after I last had them.

replies.title: Re: Stuck
replies.content:
Yes and no? Depression twines in and out of our family tree and I've had a lot of opportunity to observe it, in myself and others.

There are physical contributors to look for first: inactivity is depressing. Many medications will induce depression in some people (I cannot take codeine for this reason). I cannot for the life of me find it in the literature, but there is a very, very direct relationship between constipation and depression-- the handful of acute (and blessedly short) episodes of severe depression I've experienced have all resolved with the unclogging of my bowels-- and I have observed this in at least one family member as well. Sometimes what seems like the most intractable existential crisis can be resolved with an enema, or by drinking a glass of vegetable oil and half a Pepsi (shrugs). YMMV, obviously. Some treatable health problems (hypothyroidism comes to mind) induce depression, and getting appropriate diagnosis and treatment can be pretty close to miraculous, for the depression.

And then... sometimes it's not. The commonest features I've observed of recurring and intractable depression are, simply:

1) Inability to forgive. Basically: holding grudges. Forever. Never, ever forgetting or moving beyond any offense against oneself (or one's family or friends or pets or other membership group), real or perceived, no matter how large or small.
2) Lack of self-discipline and routine. Hard to say whether this is a cause or an effect. Probably both.

If I knew how to successfully address those things, I'd write a book on it and become a millionaire ;)

Additionally, if you are close enough to the person to understand, you are possibly to much entangled to be able to contribute to a solution and if you are farther away, on the other hand, you possibly do not understand enough of the situation, unless you are very experienced and empathetic.

Maybe it is beyond your power to solve your friends problems, who can say? Maybe the best option is to start with yourself. I know how it is to be very close to somebody suffering severe depression. Chances are very high, that your friend is not the only one who needs help. I once met somebody who I'd say was a true adept and friend of the human soul - she could help me on this issue in a profound way. If you change (and I mean CHANGE, though I didn't know what that means before it happened), this most likely will not leave your friend unaffected. After all, you are not isolated from one another and usually you can't be part of the solution if you are still part of the problem. It was not without pain to realize this.

Nachtgurke

comments.title: Creative project?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I was wondering what would be a good ritual or otherwise to help kick start writing a book?

I’ve completed the Ovate grade of CGD and practiced for over three years if that is helpful?

Many thanks

replies.title: Re: Creative project?
replies.content:
Okay, the first thing to do isn't a ritual, it's a simple practice that I recommend to all writers who haven't gotten past the need for a kickstart. It's this: every day, preferably in the morning, set a timer for fifteen minutes, get your preferred writing materials handy (pen and notebook, computer and blank Word file, or what have you) and write, nonstop, for the whole fifteen minutes. Do not let your fingers stop moving. If all you can write is "I don't know what to write, what should I write, this is really boring," and so on, write that. Do not edit or review what you've written, Just. Keep. Writing.. Do this for a few weeks and you'll find that you no longer need any kickstarting; you'll have learned the essential skill of being a writer, which is the ability to sit down and start writing at any moment.

Oh, and never edit or review while you're writing. The part of your brain the creates and the part of your brain that edits jam each other up if you try to use them at the same time; that's what "writer's block" is. If necessary, drape a towel over your screen so you can't even see what you're writing. You can clean up the typos later.

replies.title: Re: Creative project?
replies.content: Does this apply to non fiction as well as fiction ie no editing before it's done?

replies.title: Re: Creative project?
replies.content:
It's not quite that rigid in any context. For example, when I'm writing, I'll do a bunch of writing -- a scene of a novel, say -- and then take a break from the creative effort by going to another part of the novel that's already in draft, and editing and revising that. Then I'll go write another scene, and when that's finished, go edit and revise something else. The crucial thing is that you must never try to create and revise at the same time. You can do the whole rough draft and then edit, or you can create and edit by turns the way I do, but you always consciously switch from one to the other.

And yes, this applies just as much to nonfiction as to fiction.

comments.content:
In the context of pregnancy, what does occult philosophy have to say about when new life is considered a distinct "person"? (And, by extension, possibly considered worthy of protection- though that may be a separate issue. Wouldn't want to beg the question.)
Would it be at quickening, because that is when a new soul has manifested? Or perhaps not until birth, since that is when the energies separate? Or is there another point?
While the recent laws aim to ban abortion after a heartbeat, there have been other attempts at legislature that aims to ban abortion of a fetus who is "pain-capable". Interestingly, it seems like quickening and being pain-capable roughly coincide in timing.
I recognize the subject is controversial and polarizing. If this question is unwelcome, no need to post it.

replies.title: Re: Life and personhood
replies.content: This is one of those places where occult tradition fields denunciations from both sides of the current donnybrook. In occult teaching, the soul enters the fetal body at quickening -- the point 15 to 20 weeks in, when the mother can feel her baby begin voluntary movements. Before then, from an occult perspective, the fetus isn't a person yet; afterwards, it's a human being. Birth is also important, because that's when the infant receives its astrological imprint and its destiny, but every woman I know who's carried a pregnancy to term who's talked to me about this issue has mentioned that it's right around quickening that she's become very clearly aware that a person is inside her womb.

replies.title: Re: Life and personhood
replies.content: I became aware there was a NIGHT person in there.  He’d sleep all day and do acrobatics all night. I discounted the blank-slate theory of human development that was so popular in the last century.

replies.title: Re: Life and personhood
replies.content:
Questions, then, about karma and abortion:
Would it follow that abortion after quickening (a relatively small subset of abortions), is karmically analogous to terminating the life of an already-born human?
Do the beliefs of the mother and abortion provider mitigate that? (Eg if the mother and doctor dont believe the fetus is a person, does that lessen the negative karma?) I suppose the broader question here is how much is karma tied to a person's own principles, vs an outside reality.
Is karma tempered by circumstances? For instance, aborting a child because you have cancer and the treatment for it would harm the child. More broadly, is karma affected by the motives with which you take the actions?
And finally, children who met their demise in abortion- was that, possibly, due to their past life karmic burden? I ask because you mentioned Hitler likely has been reincarnated several times, with brutal ends.
Again, sensitive questions, I realize.

replies.title: Re: Life and personhood
replies.content:
Karma isn't a schoolmarm waiting to whack you across the knuckles with a ruler because you did something bad. There isn't a nice neat point scale or a karmometer you can use to grade different actions. Karma simply means that what you do shapes who you are. Everything that goes into an action -- the motives, the worldview, the circumstances, and more -- goes into the karma that results from the action.

The usual response of gurus who get questions like this is to tell their chelas to stop worrying about abstractions and spend more time meditating. All in all, that's probably good advice.

replies.title: Re: Life and personhood
replies.content:
I chortled aloud at "karmometer."
Thank you for your answers, and for redirecting me from trying to create some sort of karmic points scale.

comments.title: How do gods perceive humans?
comments.content: In the other blog you said  'If gods exist, our idiocies are being watched with raised eyebrows by beings who are smarter than we are'. I've pondered over this a lot, and over several years, but can't quite wrap my mind around it. I can see how in meditation, ritual, etc. we can raise our awareness to perceive the gods, but how does it go in reverse? Is Ishtar aware of what I'm typing right now? Do gods watch only their followers? Not to be flippant-I tend to have very concrete thinking [and yes, that's something I meditate on regularly :)] Thanks much for this blog!

replies.title: Re: How do gods perceive humans?
replies.content:
It depends on the god or goddess. Some deities traditionally perceive everything that happens in the cosmos; many pay close attention to their followers; some pay close attention to anyone doing anything that relates to what they rule -- for example, Ishtar may not be noticing what you're typing, but Nebo the god of writing very likely is!

Remember that gods aren't just a little big smarter than we are. They're vastly smarter than we are. The greatest gods are to us what we are to single-celled pond scum. Just as blue-green algae can only perceive a little bit of what's going on in the drop of water they're in, while a human being can look out over the lake and see all kinds of things the algae will never know, gods can look out over the whole cosmos and see causes and effects of which we are entirely ignorant. And, yes, they can notice what you're typing, and what every other person in the world who's writing at the same moment is writing, in every language, at a glance.

3) Camomile
4) Hyssop
5) Wormwood
6) Five Finger Grass
7) Rosemary
8) Lemons
9) Bay leaves

comments.title: Excessive solar in Golden Dawn
comments.content:
Good Morning,

Recently I have started learning/working the Lesser Hexagram Ritual from Circles of Power and I have just finished getting the Rose Cross locked down. But I am really starting to notice the heavy solar influence in the system. The Analysis of INRI being solar, solar with a side helping of solar and in both. What is the traditional way to balance that out within the system?

I had thought that maybe doing the Greater Hexagram on the day of the planet and then work through the week hitting everyone would work. Divination said slow down and don’t do that yet. As it is I have been alternating the invoking and banishing forms in the morning when I don’t work the Rose Cross instead.

On an unrelated note regarding Mr. Hughes work above. I am trying to wrap my brain around counter construction. So if you found a working for X that you wanted to counter. First you would scrutinize the ritual looking for any symbology or phrasing that could mean or really means Y. Assuming you like that Y you create a ritual to cause Y instead. Thus making Y the path of least resistance and drawing all of the energy of X ritual toward causing Y. Am I generally in the ballpark with this?

No I am not planning on doing anything like this anytime soon. I figure it is almost always better to look after your own knitting. People are going to people after all.

Thank you again for your time.
Other Dave

replies.title: Re: Excessive solar in Golden Dawn
replies.content:
The Rose Cross ritual is a solar ritual; in fact, it's solarly solar with a double helping of solar sauce and a bucket of sunshine on the side. That's the point; you use it when you need solar force and lots of it. You balance it by doing a lot of elemental work -- for example, daily practice of the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram. (The polarity here is Tiphareth and Malkuth, Sun and Earth.)

That said, you've just put your finger on the most important imbalance in the Golden Dawn system: too much of the solar influence, not enough of the other planets to balance it. That's why a certain fairly high percentage of people who receive the Adeptus Minor ritual turn into pompous jerks thereafter: if you can't embody Tiphareth in its balanced form, you embody it in its unbalanced, Qlippothic form, which means you turn into an arrogant dork who knows better than anybody else and must constantly barge in on other people's conversations to tell them how wrong they are.

As far as turning a spell, yes, that's basically what you do -- you construct a ritual that takes elements of the ritual you want to mess with, redefines those, and directs the energies toward a target much easier to influence than the one the original spell was aiming at. The best way to avoid having this done to you is not to tell anyone about your ritual work...

replies.title: Re: Excessive solar in Golden Dawn
replies.content:
Alright then. I still do the middle pillar every night and use the LRP to banish before that. What had me curious was the Analysis of INRI being present in the Hexagram ritual so that no matter what planet I am trying to work with there is the Sun as well. Now I suppose the is some imagery of all the other planets orbiting the sun.

Thank you again
Other Dave

replies.title: Re: Excessive solar in Golden Dawn
replies.content: Bingo. You want the solar influence to hold the other planetary forces in harmony, the way that the sun holds the planets in orbit.

replies.title: Re: Thievery
replies.content:
I'm going to assume that you've done all the material plane things you can do to keep the place safe. If you decide you really do want justice, the power to work with is the one you invoke for Spirit Above -- that's the power of justice in the DMH system. You will need a purple candle, on which you will scratch a circle with an equal-armed cross above itwhich you will light at dawn on seven successive Thursday mornings. Address Spirit Above the same way you do when practicing the SoP, and ask for justice to be done in this matter. Explain all the details. Then put the candle out and go about your day.

What follows is the crucial part of the spell. You must find some situation in which you yourself have been unjust to someone, and make it right, no matter how embarrassing, unpleasant, or expensive that may be. Embody justice in your own actions, and you will enable the power of justice to flow through you and affect the rest of your world. Got it? Make it happen.

Why were the US manned missions the moon named after Apollo, a solar deity? (Particularly when Apollo's sister is a perfectly good lunar deity).

Among the many missions conceived at that time was a manned journey to the Moon and back. Dr. Silverstein himself named it "Apollo" after one of the most versatile of the Greek gods. Thanks.

Steve

comments.title: 1. The Fall in Eden 2. Blue Light
comments.content:
JMG,

You mentioned in an earlier Magic Monday that the Fall of humanity in Eden is one of many dimly remembered "missteps" humanity took at its "infancy" with regard to demons. Could you list one or more of those missteps and what/which other culture's myths record of the same event?

My second question is completely unrelated to the first. [Note: the next few paragraphs is for context].

Currently I am doing a Buddha-Name chant with a rosary. Specifically referencing Medicine Buddha. As best as I can tell the Sanskrit sound of Medicine Buddha is (phonetically) Bay-shah-jay. In Tibetan Buddhism it would be Beh-Kan-Zey. While chanting his name I imagine a lapis-lazuli blue light entering the top of my head and working it's way down my spine to my perineum. I chose Medicine Buddha's name for 2 reasons - one of the people I consider a teacher for myself (via books) was the late Master Nan Huai-Chin. His essay on the considerable benefits that come from chanting any high-ranking deity/Buddha name are what convinced me to give it a shot. Found here in english translation > https://seriousbuddhism.wordpress.com/2018/09/18/nan-huai-jin-discussing-the-dharma-door-of-chanting-the-buddhas-name/

I like Master Nan because he breaks things down in a "nuts & bolts" manner. Forget about needing to "believe" in a Buddha - here's why / how it works whether you "believe" or not (according to Master Nan). [note: Sadhguru taught me it's wiser to not claim to 'believe' in anything which is not in one's own direct experience just pay attention to what's happening as its happening so I follow that advice to the best I'm able]. Also I chose Medicine Buddha because of the listed benefits of chanting his name which can be found here > https://www.buddhanet.net/pdf_file/medbudsutra.pdf. One of the benefits listed is aid in relief from pain (with proper practice, etc). His light is described as a beautiful lapis-lazuli blue that heals.

I also remember that Spring Forest Qigong (which I used to do briefly) also has you imagining a beautiful blue light coming down from the sky to enter through the crown of your head and work its way down your spine while doing the Qigong movements. Unfortunately, I didn't really stick with it though I am now thinking at this very moment that perhaps that was unwise. The founder of Spring Forest Qigong specifically claims his Qigong style is focused on healing, being as pain-free bodywise as possible and has a tagline of "a healer in every home". Obviously his Qigong dvd is cheaper than most visits to the average U.S. physician these days and its still going strong even after 25 years so I'd guess he's not doing bad financially.

I am not trying to advertise the above. I'm trying to give background info for my question. When I sit, for some reason I focus imagining this lapis lazuli light between my brows and then after a while I switch to my middle torso (this area in particular the backside has a lot of pain). Then finally my attention goes to my perineum where I've always read Muladhara chakra is.

Now...finally my question. I kind of 'get' what the Buddhist and Taoist traditions say about lapis lazuli blue light visualization benefits can be but are there any benefits that Western Occultists have noticed as well? Or does it report different experiences with blue light that have nothing to do with healing? If not, what visualization or sound or ritual do Western Occultists or Druids do for dealing with body aches and pains?

1) the Greek legend of Pandora's box and the Druid legend of the death of Einigen Gawr are said to be dim memories of that same misstep. We don't know a lot about what happened; it was a very long time ago.

2) Blue light is commonly used in Western magical practice for healing, blessing, and purification.

replies.title: Re: 1. The Fall in Eden

replies.title: Re: 1. The Fall in Eden

The main difference is that they're not so vulnerable to sustained nastiness -- we get our species' habit of pointless viciousness due to the contact with the demonic realm opened up at that point in our past.

comments.title: Abramelin word squares
comments.content:
What are your thoughts on the word squares in The Sacred Magic of Abramelin? It seems to have acquired legendary status and the mere presence of the squares is said to cause unusual and spooky events. Can these squares be adapted and used safely much as the pentacles of Solomon from the Greater Key are used in hoodoo? If not, would the 6 Or 18 in the German version) months of prayer be sufficient to work with them safely? Arw you aware of anyone using them in modern times?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Abramelin word squares
replies.content: In order to use the Abramelin squares you must perform the complete Abramelin ritual as given, to the letter, using either one of its standard forms (the six-month or the 18-month). Otherwise you're inviting demonic powers into your life when you haven't gained the ability to control and banish them. Quite a few people have used the Abramelin ritual successfully, and have had good results with the squares once they did so, but I know of way too many people who tried using them without the preliminary ritual and ended up in various kinds of trouble.

replies.title: Re: Abramelin word squares
replies.content: There's a movie on Netflix called A Dark Song about a woman who performs The Sacred Magic of Abramelin ritual.

replies.title: Re: Abramelin word squares
replies.content:
Also, the older English version of "The Sacred Magic" of Abramelin (translated by S. L. M. Mathers) was made from a defective manuscript in French, and is otherwise flawed. There are older and better manuscripts in German, and now we have newer English translations from them, too. I haven't really looked into the specific differences between the various translations myself, but they seem to be major, and they also are found in the squares.

On the other hand, there is a magician who used Mathers' translation and got excellent lasting results. His name is William Bloom, and his book has the title "The Sacred Magician: A Ceremonial Diary." There are two editions, 1976 and 1992. Each includes some material from Bloom's working journal that is omitted in the other. The second edition seems to be the easier to find, and is the one the author seems to prefer. (The first edition was issued under a pseudonym, "Georges Chevalier.")

replies.title: Re: Abramelin word squares
replies.content: I know the English version is considered flawed, but I also know a number of people who've used it with great success, so apparently the flaws don't prevent that version from doing the job!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

The concept of solar and telluric currents have been mentioned a couple of times. Are you aware of other currents? Such as a lunar or mecurial current? While I am not sure of anybody opening themselves to a venusian current, I am pretty sure that the British traditions and the Dianic threads of Wicca might willingly open themselves to a lunar thread. Would that result in excessive solipsism?

Some of my fellows and I have been having an ongoing conversation about cultural appropriation of Native American spiritual practices and the concept of 'spirit animals' which brought up two questions:
1. Appropriation aside, does it make sense that it might not even be possible to have a 'spirit animal' unless one was trained in a tradition that told you how to access one or had clan roots that had existing relationships with this spirits?
2. Does the concept of a spiritual guide exist in western mysticism or occultism other than in the New Age movement? I asked the spirits I regularly work with and each of them seems to have a personal reason for working with me but was unclear on the subject of guides.

Lastly: do banishing practices like lesser banishing of the pentagram also stir things on an etheric level? Cleansing with incense, sage, or sweeping feels different and more complete but the lesser banishing seems to impart a little of that fresh feeling.

Thank you for your consideration.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
0) The solar, telluric, and lunar/mercurial current are specific concepts from one branch of occult theory and practice with close connections to some forms of Druidry. You can use the same terminology for any of the planetary energies if you like, but it's not quite the same thing.

2) Emphatically yes. "Spirit animals" are a specific tradition within certain spiritual paths, and if you don't follow one of those paths, you probably don't have one.

2) Some Western occult traditions have the concept of a spirit guide, others do not. It really does vary.

3) Anything that affects the astral will have knock-on effects on the etheric plane, since astral patterns naturally shape the ethers. So, yes, an LBRP will have some etheric effect, though not so much as a specifically etheric working.

replies.title: lunar current
replies.content:
When I was a beginner, I was deeply involved with a version of Dianic Wicca which was strongly lunar in focus. It had lesser elements of telluric and venusian energy. I avoided working with the solar current almost entirely. This variety of Wicca emphasized connection and flow with almost no attention to developing the will and making separations. It didn't lead to solipsism on my part so much as uncertainty about where I left off and other people began.

I got help with that by joining a ceremonial magic order that had a strong solar focus. It did a great job of clarifying what was my own business and what was other people's business. I got what I wanted and thought I needed from them, and then left the organization on friendly terms.

comments.title: A Few Questions
comments.content:

replies.title: Re: A Few Questions
replies.content:
1) It may be a soul with whom you have ties from a previous life. You might consider talking to the presence and seeing what kind of answer you sense.

2) Sure. William Walker Atkinson is my other favorite New Thought writer; he wrote under a lot of pseudonyms, but The Kybalion is a good starting place for his work, and the books he wrote as Yogi Ramacharaka are a good place to go from there.

3) That does look like a positive reading. Under most circumstances I tend to warn people away from the UR Group work, but it may be well suited to you.

replies.title: Re: A Few Questions
replies.content: The same way you would pray to a god or goddess. Simply talk to them, and listen inwardly for the response (which may be nonverbal).

replies.title: Re: beginner Druid magic questions
replies.content:
1) You can do either one, but the more general one -- the second in your example -- is better for everyday use, especially for novices.

2) You're doing it the same way I do -- breathing in through the front of the body -- so that's probably okay. ;-)

comments.title: Lower astral-plane entities
comments.content: I know you've mentioned from time to time the very thick lower astral-plane energy that has been building up in a big way since about 2016. I am curious if you share my belief that conscious negative entities (I suppose one could call them "demons" if one were so predisposed) on the lower astral plane are deliberately cultivating all this bad energy (fear, hate, hysteria, etc.) so that they can feast upon it?

replies.title: Re: Lower astral-plane entities
replies.content: That's a standard teaching in occult philosophy. Astral beings range from the demonic to the angelic; all of them radiate what, to us, appears in the form of emotional energy (the astral plane is also called the Desire World in some sources, as it's the plane of emotions and desires), and we resonate with whatever level of the astral we're attuned to. Combine that with the buildup of grubby energies at the bottom of the lower astral, and the fact that the beings in question are conscious and have wills of their own, and yeah, that's one of the things that happens.
